assessment_refusal.reason_for_refusal.answer:
I am requesting that my daughter, Eleanor Thompson, be excused from all state-required standardized assessments listed below for the 2025–2026 school year. This decision comes after several years of observing how high-stakes testing has affected her anxiety, her attitude toward school, and the amount of instructional time devoted to test preparation rather than deeper learning.

Eleanor is a strong reader and generally does well academically, but in the weeks leading up to the MCA test windows she becomes noticeably stressed: she has trouble sleeping, complains of stomachaches on testing days, and becomes very focused on whether she will "mess up" the test and disappoint her teachers. We have discussed this with her classroom teacher and school counselor in previous years. They have been supportive and helpful, but ultimately the structure of the testing schedule still causes Eleanor significant worry.

In addition, we have concerns about the amount of classroom time that is redirected toward practicing test-taking strategies and working through practice items that closely mirror the MCA format. While we understand the district’s accountability requirements, as parents we prioritize instruction that emphasizes problem-solving, creativity, project-based learning, and extended writing over multiple-choice testing. We feel that the current testing regime narrows the curriculum and puts disproportionate emphasis on a single measure of performance on a single set of days.

We also have questions about how the test data are used. From our experience, the results arrive months after the tests are taken and do not provide sufficiently detailed or timely feedback to help us support Eleanor’s learning in specific, actionable ways. Her classroom assessments, teacher feedback, and report cards have been more useful to us for understanding her strengths and areas where she needs support.

Finally, Eleanor has a history of mild generalized anxiety that we manage with her pediatrician and a child therapist. High-stakes testing environments (timed, computer-based, quiet rooms with rigid rules) tend to exacerbate her symptoms. We would like to avoid triggering those symptoms when there are alternative ways for the school to measure her progress, such as classroom-based assessments, portfolio work, and teacher observations.

For these reasons—concern about Eleanor’s mental well-being, disagreement with the emphasis placed on high-stakes standardized tests, limited usefulness of the test results for instructional purposes, and a preference for more holistic measures of learning—we are opting her out of participation in statewide assessments for this school year. We understand that this may affect how the school and district report participation rates and that Eleanor may receive an automatic "not proficient" or similar designation for accountability purposes. We accept those consequences and still believe that opting out is in her best interest at this time.
